text: Hi, I'm Adi from AWS.
Hi, I'm Niraj from IDA Technologies and this is my architecture.
Niraj, today we'll be talking about IDA Technologies Fraud Waste Abuse or FWA in short, as a service.
Can you tell us a bit about IDA technology and what are the challenges that you faced that led you to this architecture?
Thank you, Adi. Yes, in IDA Technologies we develop solutions for health insurance companies.
We help them to solve Fraud Waste Abuse identification present in their claims as well as claims adjudications.
The challenges which we used to face with health insurance is the overall turnaround time when we
want to demonstrate the system and deploy the system to clients, it takes almost months.
The challenge is how we can reduce this time and that's where we decided to develop a FWA SaaS service
which we will be looking at here.
A SaaS service, I can't wait to get into the architecture. Can you talk us through it?
Yes. So this FWA SaaS service exposes a web UI service which users can log in. So once users
are logged in into the system, what they can do, they can upload a file which usually comprises of
number of claims which should they want to identify the presence of FWA. And once this file is uploaded,
it is notified to AWS Step Functions where it orchestrates the processing of that file. And that
orchestration, what it does, it triggers the AWS Fargate where our machine learning model is running,
which identifies the presence of Fraud Waste Abuse present in the claims. And it processes all those
claims and stores it into the database. As well as once this processing is complete, the user is also
notified for the completion of this file so that they can come back into the UI and look at the
dashboard where all the beautiful results are present for their analysis.
So you effectively have put a serverless web UI in front of your AI ML model here on Fargate. But
you did mention it took you months to implement this solution on your customer site, which I can only
assume is an on-prem deployment. So that makes me curious, why Fargate instead of something like EC2,
which closely resembles your on-prem deployment? So there are two reasons which I say is like one is
with Fargate because it's a managed container service, the developer productivity will be high,
we can directly go and deploy containers. And the other space is like the cost effectiveness. We spin up
the AWS Fargate task when a file is uploaded. So it's like on need basis, compute is required and then
once it is done, it is released. That makes a lot of sense, yeah, especially on the cost efficiency.
Now, what about those times when the insurance providers, they would have got their existing claims
management system? So I would have thought it's highly unlikely for an insurance company to have
someone like this user here, who download the claim from the existing system and then upload it to a
web UI. How would you handle this scenario? Yeah, you're absolutely right on that use case. So what
we have done, if you look into this system, we can high level, we can say like, this was a bad service,
where users are uploading a file. The other case which you mentioned through integration system is
the API cases. What we have done is we have exposed an API through Amazon API Gateway. It's a REST API where
client systems can make an API call through the API keys allotted to them. As it is hosted in API
Gateway, they provide its own usage plans also. So once they have passed the data in the form of JSON,
this is further forwarded to AWS Lambda where the same machine learning model is running which we hosted in
AWS Fargate. So what it does, it quickly go through that particular claim, identify the presence of the
FWA into it, stores the result, as well as it returns it back to the client system which it triggers.
So that the client system consumes the result and further process it into their downstream if required.
I see. So you've got a batch interface, real-time interface. Now you did mention one thing that sparks my
curiosity. The same code were deployed in Lambda and Fargate. Doesn't that mean code duplication?
No, it's not code duplication here. We are deploying the same code across these two AWS Fargate and Lambda.
What we do is like it's the same container which it is deployed. It's just a matter of configuration
which makes it compatible for both the services. Ah, so same container image deployed on Lambda.
Yes. We are using the same code. But still, those are like two different compute services. Why not just
Fargate or Lambda? So there is a reason behind it. As I mentioned, the above case we classify it as a
batch and here it is a real-time. So in real-time they need instance response. In batch, there will be
a lot of data which will be coming in. So the reason behind using AWS Fargate and Lambda, in Lambda we have
a timeout of 15 minutes max. So we don't want to cross the threshold. So that's where AWS Lambda helps us in
real-time case. Whereas AWS Fargate here, the easiest task will run till all the claims are processed.
Makes sense. Makes sense. What about data here? Like how do you isolate data amongst your customers?
Data is isolated here in the form of schemas. So what we do here is whenever a client is on with it,
a dedicated schema is created for them. And any cross-tenant schema accesses are not allowed. So when a request
arrives, it have permission to access data within its own schema. Ah, that's very reassuring. Now what are some
of the benefits that you've realized after this? So as I mentioned earlier, the main challenge for us
was like overall turnaround time. Now it is reduced from months to days. It's a matter of time client
on boards and they start to use the system. And of course, serverless technologies are here. So
definitely cost, it's cost-effective solution. Amazing. Thank you for sharing your architecture with us.
And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
